Outline how species diversity and population size influence the resilience of an ecosystem.
Describe the similarities and differences in using a biotic index and a diversity index to assess ecosystems.
With reference to named examples, discuss the significance of diversity in the sustainability of food production systems.
greater species diversity/greater population size usually lead to greater resilience
column2: with more species, it is more likely others can take over the role/niche of any lost/declining species
column3: more food chains/energy/biogeochemical pathways in an ecosystem provides redundancy therefore greater stability
column4: a variety of species is more likely to include those resistant to environmental change
column5: larger populations provide greater storages that can last over periods of lower productivity
column6: larger populations generally carry greater genetic diversity
column7: larger populations of invasive species may lead to reduced diversity/resilience
column8: lower populations are more prone to extinction after a disturbance (e.g. habitat fragmentation) / or due to stochastic fluctuations
column9: an ecosystem may be more resilient if there are many small populations of different species than one large population of a single dominating species
column10: large populations of foundation/keystone species may be crucial for resilience of certain ecosystems (e.g. corals, kelp, beavers, elephants, pines, hemlock)

column1:
Note: Award credit to any valid arguments providing they directly relate species diversity or population size to resilience.
Award [3] max for responses addressing only diversity or only population size.

column2: both may involve quantitative sampling/estimating the abundance of living organisms
column3: both require multiple samples for effective comparisons/reliability
column4: both indices involve evaluating the range/variety of different species
column5: both involve calculating a single figure (index) from a collection of data

column1:
Differences:
only biotic indices (not diversity indices) give different values to sensitive and tolerant species
column2: biotic indices are specifically used for evaluating impact of pollution whereas diversity indices just measure variety and evenness of species/general condition/maturity of an ecosystem
column3: diversity indices are appropriate for most ecosystems whereas biotic indices are usually for aquatic systems
column4: biotic indices usually focus on animal species/macroinvertebrates alone whereas diversity indices can be used for plant or animal communities
column5: diversity indices usually address all species present in system whereas biotic index focuses on a particular selection

column1:

column3: EIs involve the setting of measurable goals from established baseline measurements
column4: EIs can be used at a range of scales from local to global
column5: after some time, measures can be reassessed and compared to the baselines/domestic objectives/international agreements (eg Kyoto/Montreal Protocol)
column6: environmental impact assessments (EIAs) are EIs that measure a wide profile of indicators usually before and after some development

column12: loss of these sensitive species may be an effective indicator for appealing to the public
column13: some indicator species are particularly resistant to pollution so a high abundance/dominance of these species may suggest high pollution (eg tubifex worms/coliform bacteria).

To what extent might it be better to consider environmental issues at a global, rather than a local level, when planning for sustainable development in Siberia?
Arguments that support addressing issue at global level [4 max]:
<li>many of the issues generate global impacts / affect ecological services to whole planet

<li>e.g. deforestation affects climate change / widely different ecosystems, e.g. oceans / trees are exported around the world, making deforestation in Siberia part of the global/importing countries ecological footprint
<li>loss of habitats/local species e.g. tiger affect global biodiversity
<li>it is a moral duty/responsibility of humans to conserve species/biodiversity globally
<li>local habitats/populations are quite small/vulnerable/less resilient and so more easily lost
<li>many of the impacts are stimulated by global markets/economies/demands
<li>i.e. international demand for goods, e.g. timber/tiger parts/fossil fuels/metals
<li>so international management is required because source/impact of problem is international
<li>experts from around the world can collaborate to help tackle issues/plan for sustainable development
<li>countries can help each other with resources/expertise
<li>at global level there is more access to funds to implement strategies
<li>countries need to collaborate to avoid tragedy of the commons, eg climate change
Arguments that support addressing issue at local level [4 max]:
<li>some issues are more localized, e.g. acid deposition / cause and effect of acid deposition is local / discharge of effluent affects local river quality/biodiversity
<li>implementation and enforcement needs to be done at a local level/on the ground (e.g. policing of poachers/reduction in emissions of NO<sub>x</sub>/SO<sub>2</sub>/CO)
<li>most management strategies are more successful when they engage local support / decisions at global level may not take into account local customs/needs/knowledge and alienate local people
<li>at a global level it is more difficult to get everyone to agree/take action

Conclusion/opinion [1 max]: e.g. âdue to the interconnected nature of environmental systems, action needs to be taken at a global level e.g. through international agreements, however these then need to be implemented at a local level to be successfulâ

Note: An isolated statement/opinion, e.g. âEnvironmental issues are best addressed at a global levelâ should not be considered as a valid conclusion/opinion without supporting evidence.
Award [5 max] if there is no conclusion/opinion.

Distinguish between two named biomes and the factors that cause their distribution.
Evaluate one method for measuring primary productivity in a named ecosystem.
Discuss how human activities impact the flows and stores in the nitrogen cycle.

Distinguishing features [3 max]:
tundra has lower insolation / TRF has the highest insolation of all biomes
column2: TRF has constant insolation throughout the year / tundra has long, dark winters
column3: tundra has lower mean annual temperature / TRF has warmer temperatures
column4: tundra has 6â10 months of freezing temperatures / TRF has constant warm temperatures
column5: TRF has the largest annual precipitation compared to any biome / tundra precipitation is as low as in deserts
column6: TRF has almost constant precipitation throughout the year
column7: tundra precipitation mostly in form of snow / tundra has a characteristic layer of frozen ground below the surface/permafrost
column8: TRFs have the highest biodiversity of all biomes

column1:
Explanation of distribution: [2 max]
tundra found at the poles, while TRFs at the tropics (above and below the equator)
column2: Hadley cell rises at the Equator causing huge precipitation (low pressure zone) / as warm air cools and its moisture condenses
column3: tundra is found in the low pressure area of polar cell / at a region where there is net loss of solar energy (causing freezing temperatures) / alpine tundra found on mountaintops, where temperatures are below 0 most time of the year due to high altitude

column1:
Notes: Award [3] max for correctly indentified distinguishing features i.e. insolation / temperature / precipitation / permafrost / high biodiversity etc.
Award [2] max for explanation of distribution.
Award [2] max overall if no biomes are named.

Alternative 1:
Method:
light and dark bottle for an aquatic ecosystem
column2: measure dissolved oxygen at start and end of experiment
column3: compare measurements in a transparent (with light) and opaque (without light) bottle containing sample of water from ecosystem
column4: net productivity is equivalent to change in dissolved oxygen in light bottle
column5: gross productivity is equivalent to change in dissolved oxygen in light bottle plus the loss of dissolved oxygen in the dark bottle (due to respiration)
column6: measurements taken for a set period of time, eg one week

column2: ethical method â samples can be returned to ecosystem
column3: difficult to isolate primary producers from consumers in ecosystem sample
column4: only collecting productivity for submerged subset of ecosystem
column5: measurements dependent upon temperature
column6: quality of measurements depends on precision of instruments

column1:
Alternative 2:
Method:
three comparison plots, one covered in opaque plastic for terrestrial ecosystem
column2: measure dry biomass at start and end of experiment
column3: compare measurements from an open (with light) and covered (without light) plot in ecosystem
column4: net productivity is equivalent to change in biomass in open plot
column5: gross productivity is equivalent to change in biomass plus the loss of biomass in the covered plot (due to respiration)
column6: measurements taken for a set period of time, eg one week

column1:
Evaluation:
easy to isolate primary producers in ecosystem sample
column3: ethical problems as samples need to be killed to measure dry biomass
column4: measurements dependent upon temperature
column5: productivity easier to measure in simpler systems
column6: difficult to measure with larger producers/trees

column1:
Notes: Award [3] max for description of method. Evaluation should address inherent strengths and weaknesses of method. Do not credit examples of poor execution eg inaccurate measurement/samples too small etc.
Award [6] max if ecosystem is not named.
No credit can be given to responses that mistakenly address eg biodiversity rather than productivity.

Outline how species diversity and population size influence the resilience of an ecosystem.
Describe the similarities and differences in using a biotic index and a diversity index to assess ecosystems.
With reference to named examples, discuss the significance of diversity in the sustainability of food production systems.
greater species diversity/greater population size usually lead to greater resilience
column2: with more species, it is more likely others can take over the role/niche of any lost/declining species
column3: more food chains/energy/biogeochemical pathways in an ecosystem provides redundancy therefore greater stability
column4: a variety of species is more likely to include those resistant to environmental change
column5: larger populations provide greater storages that can last over periods of lower productivity
column6: larger populations generally carry greater genetic diversity
column7: larger populations of invasive species may lead to reduced diversity/resilience
column8: lower populations are more prone to extinction after a disturbance (e.g. habitat fragmentation) / or due to stochastic fluctuations
column9: an ecosystem may be more resilient if there are many small populations of different species than one large population of a single dominating species
column10: large populations of foundation/keystone species may be crucial for resilience of certain ecosystems (e.g. corals, kelp, beavers, elephants, pines, hemlock)

column1:
Note: Award credit to any valid arguments providing they directly relate species diversity or population size to resilience.
Award [3] max for responses addressing only diversity or only population size.

column2: both may involve quantitative sampling/estimating the abundance of living organisms
column3: both require multiple samples for effective comparisons/reliability
column4: both indices involve evaluating the range/variety of different species
column5: both involve calculating a single figure (index) from a collection of data

column1:
Differences:
only biotic indices (not diversity indices) give different values to sensitive and tolerant species
column2: biotic indices are specifically used for evaluating impact of pollution whereas diversity indices just measure variety and evenness of species/general condition/maturity of an ecosystem
column3: diversity indices are appropriate for most ecosystems whereas biotic indices are usually for aquatic systems
column4: biotic indices usually focus on animal species/macroinvertebrates alone whereas diversity indices can be used for plant or animal communities
column5: diversity indices usually address all species present in system whereas biotic index focuses on a particular selection

column1:
Note: Award [4] max if only similarities or only differences are given.
Only credit differences where both sides of the contrast are given or clearly implicit. No credit for naming indices eg Simpsonâs, Trent etc.

column2: polar cells) creates patterns of climate that determine dominant vegetation types
column3: low pressure due to intense heating/high insolation at the equator causes / rising moist air in the tropics creates high precipitation giving rise to rainforests
column4: moving polewards (at high altitude), air cools, becomes denser and sinks forming a high-pressure zone / descending/dry air (20â30Â° latitude/tropics) creates water-limiting/arid conditions giving rise to deserts
column5: some of the air continues towards the poles to equalize temperature difference / atmosphere transfers heat from (sub-)tropics to mid-latitudes giving rise to temperate biomes
column6: descending/dry air (high latitude/polar regions) creates water-limiting conditions in tundra
column7: water vapour (from mid-latitudes/temperate regions) is transferred to high latitudes giving rise to heavy precipitation/snow in boreal forest
column8: water vapour is transferred from ocean surfaces overland generating freshwater aquatic systems
column9: prevailing winds/jet streams (blowing from high to low pressure) bring precipitation to a region, e.g. temperate rainforest in mountainous region/riverine/water-margin systems
column10: rain shadow effect of high mountains causes dry winds in the leeward side, resulting in arid or semi-arid biomes (e.g. Tibetan Plateau, Mongolian Gobi desert and steppes)
column11: atmosphere may be responsible for shifting biomes due to global warming/climate change

The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although âESS terminologyâ has been conflated with âUnderstanding conceptsâ). This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band.

understanding concepts and terminology of steady state and dynamic equilibria, stability, resilience, tipping-points, diversity, storage size, productivity, negative/positive feedback, complexity, community interdependence/interaction, human impacts of overexploitation/unsustainable harvesting, pollution, habitat degradation/destruction, unsustainable development, etc

breadth in addressing and linking factors that provide stability/resilience egÂ storage size, diversity, productivity, complexity, etc with human activities that either weaken/reduce them eg overfishing, intensive agriculture, C emissions/global warming, water/atmospheric pollution, deforestation, urbanisation, mining/resource extraction, etc or activities that strengthen/protect them eg legislation setting pollution limits/standards/fishing quotas, sustainable development schemes, alternative/renewable energies, conservation efforts/reserves, etc

examples of specific negative impacts on stability of ecological systems e.g.Â overfishing reduces the storage size of targeted fish populations reducing their stability, C emissions lead to climate change that reduces primary productivity of systems, eutrophication will interfere with negative feedback loops in freshwater systems, atmospheric pollutants may eliminate sensitive species of lichen reducing diversity, etc and positive impacts on stability e.g. in-situ conservation protects complexity of relationships in system, agricultural techniques conserving soils maintain high productivity, etc
balanced analysis of the extent to which human activities promote or prevent destabilisation of ecological systems with acknowledgement of relevant counter-arguments/alternative viewpoints.

a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given eg âThe role of humans in destabilising ecological systems is very diverse, and with the current size and growth of population, the magnitude of that role is becoming immense, calling for urgent and wide-scale efforts to adopt a proactive role in re-stabilising those systems.â NB This is only an example of a possible conclusion. The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although âESS terminologyâ has been conflated with âUnderstanding conceptsâ). This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band.

understanding concepts and terminology of equilibria, sustainability, natural capital/income, climatic factors (temp/precipitation/seasonality), greenhouse gases, climate change, biome shifts, water conservation, irrigation, desertification, vegetarian vs meat-rich diets, mitigation, adaptation, commercial vs artisanal, intensive vs extensive, food miles, selective breeding/genetic engineering, etc

breadth in addressing and linking influences of climate on food production eg water scarcity, shifting biomes, mean temperatures / precipitation, desertification, wind / rain / erosion, etc and influences of food production on climate eg methane production, deforestation, use of fossil fuels, global transport, etc and ways in which production strategies may adapt to, or mitigate climate change

examples of food production strategies that adapt to climate change eg water conservation, drip irrigation, terracing, drought/temperature resistant crops, aquaponics, greenhouses, etc and strategies that mitigate climate change eg switching from meat-rich diets, localising food production, employing artisanal/low-energy farming strategies, etc
balanced analysis of the extent to which production strategies from a range of contexts may contribute to, or mitigate against, an equilibrium between food production and the climate etc

a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given eg âalthough there are many production strategies that mitigate or adapt to climate change, the relationship has already shifted so far away from a sustainable equilibrium, and with growing populations, it seems unlikely that their contribution will be sufficient to avoid a tipping point in the futureâ

column1:
Montreal:
In November 2015, the City of Montreal discharged between 5 and 8 billion litres of untreated sewage into the St Lawrence River.
• It was called âFlushGateâ in the Canadian news.
• Citizens were warned to avoid contact with the water.
• River pollution levels returned to normal within 4 to 10 days.
• The Mayor of Montreal described it as the âmost environmentally-friendly solutionâ.

Quebec:
In November 2016, the City of Quebec discharged 110 million litres of untreated sewage into the St Lawrence River.
• 25â% of Canadians do not have access to sewage or wastewater treatment centres.
• 205 billion litres of untreated sewage are released into Canadian rivers and oceans each year.

The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although âESS terminologyâ has been conflated with âUnderstanding conceptsâ). This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band.

understanding concepts and terminology of environmental value systems, biodiversity, habitat/species/genetic diversity, ecocentric/anthropocentric/technocentric, bio-rights, intrinsic value, stewardship, conservation areas/national parks, in-situ/ex-situ conservation, species vs habitat approach, ecotourism

examples of environmental value systems eg deep ecologists, ecocentric, anthropocentric, environmental managers, technocentric, cornucopian, and approaches to conservation eg habitat/in-situ conservation, ex-situ zoos/breeding programmes, ecotourism, gene banks/genetic engineering/in vitro development, flagship species

• The Coral Triangle Initiative on Coral Reefs, Fisheries and Food Security (CTI-CFF) is an agreement between all six nations in the area with the objectives of:

column2: >ensuring food security and a sustainable livelihood of residents of the Coral Triangle
>protecting the unique ecosystem and marine species within the Coral Triangle.

column1: • Conservation projects in the Coral Triangle involve collaboration between national governments, regional organisations, non-governmental organisations, private companies and local communities.
• Popular approaches to encourage sustainable use of the sea and its resources include creation of Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) and networks of Locally Managed Marine Areas (LMMAs).
• MPAs are usually managed by national and local government whereas LMMAs are more likely to be managed by the local community.

• These designated areas are often classified according to their use or the restrictions placed on them, such as âno-take zonesâ where no fishing is allowed or âtourism zonesâ that can be used for recreation but where other activities such as fishing are banned.
• Some zones allow restricted activity, such as use by local fishermen using only traditional fishing methods or during certain times of the year.
• Research has shown that over time in a well-managed MPA there is an increase in fish size, fish numbers and overall biodiversity.
• There are currently over 1000 MPAs within the Coral Triangle.
• The Coral Triangle Initiative aims to designate 20 % of each of the main habitats within the Coral Triangle as MPAs by 2020.

column1: • Beavers build dams with sticks, creating ponds and slowing the flow of rivers.

column1: • Marsh forms at the edge of beaver ponds.

• Grasses and sedges colonise the nutrient-rich silt at the bottom of the former pond, creating a beaver
    meadow.

• Beaver meadows have more light penetration, higher soil moisture, more nitrogen and a different
    vegetation from that of the adjacent river-edge forest.
• Dams may collapse because of heavy rains or spring melt, or if beaver is killed by a predator.

With reference to named examples, distinguish between a primary and secondary pollutant.
Explain how organic waste may be an effective fertilizer in terrestrial systems but a source of pollution in aquatic systems.
To what extent can different environmental value systems contribute to both causing and resolving the problem of water scarcity?
a primary pollutant is one which is active on emission / directly impacts the environment
column2: eg CO2 is released from burning fossil fuels and actively contributes to global warming / CFCs are released from aerosols and actively contribute to ozone depletion
column3: a secondary pollutant is one formed from a primary pollutant through physical/chemical change
column4: eg CO2 combines with sea water to form carbonic acid that leads to impacts on calciferous shelled organisms or corals / NOx combines with water to form acid precipitation / NO2 forms PAN/ozone (that contributes to photochemical smog).

column1:
Award [2 max] if no examples are given.
Examples of primary pollutants need to include their direct impact (eg NOx can be either primary or secondary without such specification) and examples of secondary need to include the process leading to their pollutionary impact).
Award [1 max] for example of primary, and [1 max] for example of secondary.

In terrestrial systems [4 max]:
organic waste such as cattle manure/compost can be added to soil as fertilizer
column2: its decomposition releases nitrates/phosphates/nutrients that promote plant growth
column3: the slow release will help to prevent run-off/eutrophication/red tide/algal bloom of water bodies
column4: improves soil quality/structure / making it less prone to erosion/compaction / increases plowability
column5: it is similar to the natural organic waste in a terrestrial system so may not be a pollutant.

column1:
In aquatic systems [4 max]:
organic waste eg sawdust/domestic sewage/agricultural run-off can be discharged into aquatic systems
column2: increases turbidity/floats on surface/algal growth reduces light penetration and primary productivity of system
column3: its decomposition leads to bacterial absorption of O2 / adds high BOD reducing O2 availability
column4: this is a limiting factor in aquatic (not terrestrial) systems (negatively) impacting aquatic life
column5: (decomposition also) releases nitrates/phosphates/nutrients leading to eutrophication/red tide/algal blooms
column6: domestic sewage/sawmill effluent may contain non-biodegradable organic pollutants/toxins/pathogens
column7: can pollute drinking water sources for human populations/animal species.

• The desalination plant now supplies water to the existing Uranium mines in the area, and has spare capacity that could be used to provide water to the communities in the Swakop Valley and to the proposed Husab mine.

Outline how energy drives the hydrological cycle.

column2: >Explain, with the use of a system diagram, how human activities affect flows in the global water cycle.

column1: To what extent do the approaches and strategies of different environmental value systems improve access to fresh water?
primary source of energy driving hydrological cycle is solar energy
column2: solar energy causes changes of state in water eg evaporation/sublimation/melting/transpiration
column3: condensation of water releases (latent) heat energy causing air to rise/hurricanes
column4: solar energy causes the temperature differences that create winds/cause movement of water vapour in the atmosphere/advection
column5: kinetic/wind energy moves clouds (from ocean over land, usually)
column6: solar energy melts ice producing streams/lakes/rising ocean levels
column7: solar energy causes both transfers (eg advection) and transformations (eg evaporation) in hydrological cycle
column8: precipitation/run-off/streamflow occurs due to gravitational energy/difference in potential energy between the high and low positions
column9: transpiration is driven by opening of leaf stomata which is due to chemical energy (cellular respiration in mitochondria).

column8: warmer/polluted streams (5) due to thermal/toxic pollution from industrial effluent

Outline, using examples, the differences between primary and secondary pollution.
Explain the causes and effects of acid deposition on natural ecosystems.
To what extent is pollution impacting human food production systems?

Primary pollution:
is active upon emission of pollutant

Secondary pollution:
occurs when primary pollutants undergo some kind of physical or chemical change
column2: nitrogen/sulphur oxides combining with water to form nitric acid/sulphur acid/acid rain
column3: nitrogen oxides/VOCs combining to form tropospheric ozone/photochemical smog

column1: Note: some pollutants (eg ozone/sulphur oxides) can be credited as both a primary and secondary pollutant but to gain credit for the latter there must be an identification of a primary pollutant from which it is derived.
Do not accept eutrophication (eg toxic substances released from algal blooms) as example of secondary pollutant.

column2: the sources of these are mainly coal-burning industries/transportation/electricity generation
column3: emissions from livestock/use &
column4: production of inorganic fertilizers also contribute
column5: volcanic eruptions can also cause acid rain/release oxides of N/S
column6: NOx/SOx react with water to form acid deposition
column7: this acid may be deposited locally as dry deposition or dissolve in air moisture and reach ground by wet precipitation
column8: nitrous oxides produce nitric acid / sulphur oxides produce sulphuric acid
column9: wind may carry primary pollutants causing ecological damage to be widespread.

column1:
Effects:
direct adverse effect of acidity on living organisms eg kills lichens / plankton / fish / soil microbiota
column2: causing leaf-fall/thinning of waxy cuticle/reduced immunity to disease/root damage in terrestrial plants
column3: leading to reduced primary production/plant growth
column4: indirect toxic effect by changing chemistry of soil/water
column5: eg increased solubility/leaching of plant nutrients/reduced soil fertility
column6: eg increased solubility/release of toxic metals/aluminium damaging fish/plants
column7: such toxic metals might undergo bioconcentration/biomagnification
column8: overall, may therefore cause loss of biodiversity/reduction in food chains/webs etc.

column1:
Note: Do not credit biomagnification except in the explicit context of releasing heavy metals. And do not credit impacts of ocean acidification or impacts on limestone/urban infrastructure which are beyond the limits of this question.
Award [5 max] if either causes or effects are not included in response.

Outline how energy drives the hydrological cycle.

column2: >Explain, with the use of a system diagram, how human activities affect flows in the global water cycle.

column1: To what extent do the approaches and strategies of different environmental value systems improve access to fresh water?
primary source of energy driving hydrological cycle is solar energy
column2: solar energy causes changes of state in water eg evaporation/sublimation/melting/transpiration
column3: condensation of water releases (latent) heat energy causing air to rise/hurricanes
column4: solar energy causes the temperature differences that create winds/cause movement of water vapour in the atmosphere/advection
column5: kinetic/wind energy moves clouds (from ocean over land, usually)
column6: solar energy melts ice producing streams/lakes/rising ocean levels
column7: solar energy causes both transfers (eg advection) and transformations (eg evaporation) in hydrological cycle
column8: precipitation/run-off/streamflow occurs due to gravitational energy/difference in potential energy between the high and low positions
column9: transpiration is driven by opening of leaf stomata which is due to chemical energy (cellular respiration in mitochondria).

column1: Award up to [4 max] for identifying following impacts either on the diagram or in words:
melting (1) will increase due to global warming/climate change/increased greenhouse gas emissions
column2: freezing (2) will decrease due to global warming/climate change/etc
column3: precipitation (4,6,8) will be polluted due to acid formation/emissions of NOx &
column4: SO<sub>x</sub>/ combustion of coal
column5: precipitation (4,6,8) could change/increase/decrease due to climate change/etc
column6: percolation/infiltration (8) will decrease due to urbanisation / soil compaction caused by eg overgrazing
column7: run-off (5) will increase due to urbanization/deforestation
column8: warmer/polluted streams (5) due to thermal/toxic pollution from industrial effluent
column9: evaporation (3,7,9) will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc
column10: evapotranspiration (10) will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc
column11: evapotranspiration (10) will be reduced due to deforestation
column12: uptake by plants (11) will be reduced due to deforestation / urbanization / long fallow periods
column13: groundwater flow/aquifers (12) will change dependent on climatic impacts/precipitation / rate of human extraction
column14: cloud seeding increases precipitation (4,6,8) (addressing problem of drought/fog around airports).

The figure refers to a typical Western European diet. This example shows recommended consumption of food types (on the basis of health) and the environmental impact of their production.
With reference to Figures 1(a) and 1(b) state the food that has the highest environmental impact.
With reference to Figures 1(a) and 1(b) state the food that has the highest recommended consumption.
Describe the relationship between both pyramids in Figures 1(a) and 1(b).
Identify two environmental impacts associated with producing the foods near the base of the recommended consumption pyramid (Figure 1(a)).
Describe how foods high on the environmental impact pyramid, shown in Figure 1(b), are likely to affect the ecological footprint of global food production.
Outline two reasons why the composition of a typical diet in other regions of the world may differ from the Western European diet shown in Figure 1(a).
generally, the foods with the higher level of recommended consumption have a lower environmental impact
column2: foods at the âtopâ (pictorially, rather than both apices) of both pyramids tend to be from higher trophic levels/produce a higher ecological footprint (EF) / foods at âbottomâ of both pyramids tend to be from lower trophic levels/primary producers/produce a lower EF
column3: pyramids appear opposite to one another / have an inverse relationship/negative correlation

column1: use of pesticides/herbicides causing soil degradation / superbugs / superweeds
column2: over use of fertilizers causing soil degradation / eutrophication
column3: over-cultivation of land causing soil degradation
column4: intensive irrigation/over use of water leading to water scarcity / salinization
column5: mono-cropping reducing biodiversity/increasing risk of losses through disease
column6: use of fossil fuels through mechanization/intense farming/food miles that release GHGs/CO2
column7: methane released from rice-farming

column1:
Credit can be given to any valid impact of agricultural food production. 
Credit may be given for positive impacts (e.g. roots prevent soil erosion/crop rotation enriches soils) but not just the absence of negative impacts (e.g. less methane/less water, etc.)

Ecological footprint will be greater becauseâ¦
higher trophic levels/more energy lossâ¦
column2: â¦greater land area required (to produce same quantity of food)
column3: more dependence on fossil fuels/mechanisation/food miles (+ release of GHG/CO2)
column4: release of methane from cattle increases global greenhouse gas concentration
column5: higher water consumption when farming animals
column6: overgrazing leading to loss of arable land

column1:
Award [1 max] if INCREASE in ecological footprint/land area required is not mentioned.
Credit may be given for any valid impact on EF due to production of food high on environmental impact (beef/pork/poultry/fish/cheese/olive oil).

Their choice may be influenced byâ¦
traditional/cultural/religious values of certain foods
column3: availability of water supply
column4: available technology/expertise for certain cultivation techniques
column5: wealth / relative cost of production
column6: some countries/cultures may have different perceptions of what constitutes health/healthy eating
column7: some cultures may consider environmental impacts/have EVS that influences choice of food production
column8: some LEDCs may not be able to import foods so rely on more local foods

The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may beÂ offered in responses. understanding concepts and terminology of carrying capacity, ecological footprint, waste assimilation, recycling/re-use/reduction strategies, BOD, toxicity, greenhouse gases, global warming/climate change, selective breeding/genetic engineering, commercial vs artisanal farming, mono-cropping, soil quality, desertification, Malthus/Boserup theories, etc

breadth in addressing and linking a range of ways in which different sources of waste eg agricultural, domestic, industrial, transport etc and food production systems eg commercial, polyculture, artisanal, etc impact/deplete available resources thus influencing carrying capacity for humans, etc

examples of how carrying capacity may be influenced through wastes eg toxicity to natural populations, increasing BOD in aquatic systems, greenhouse gases leading to global warming/climate change reducing (or possibly increasing?) productivity, and through food production eg use of pesticides reducing natural populations, reducing soil quality through overharvesting/compaction/irrigation, habitat destruction, etc and examples of how these influences may be reduced through waste management strategies eg recycling, etc, alternative energy, and alternative agricultural practices eg polyculture, crop rotation, more efficient crops/GMOs, etc
balanced analysis of the net impact of waste and food production on the carrying capacity for humans etc

a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given eg âAlthough there are many mitigating strategies that can reduce the influence of waste and food production on carrying capacity, overall, an increasing per capita footprint of humans will inevitably result in a reduced carrying capacity.â

Water stress is the total annual extraction of water as a proportion of the renewable supply in a given area. If the extraction represents 40 % or more of the available supply it is described as a high risk area.
State the crop that is under the greatest water stress.
Identify two strategies that could be used to grow crops in areas of high water stress.
Identify three factors that may lead to an increase in water stress.

column2: use crop cultivars that require less water (eg drought resistant seeds/GMOs) / replace high stress crops (eg cotton) with low stress (eg oats)
column3: improve water catchment/storage with dams
column4: reservoirs
column5: install desalinization plants in coastal areas
column6: plant cover crops/use terracing (to reduce evaporation/run-off losses)
column7: use salt-tolerant crops to exploit available sea-water/saline soils.

column1: Do not credit simply âirrigationâ. Too vague.
increased demand through increased population / domestic/agricultural/industrial use
column2: increased water consumption through more affluent lifestyles / shift to meat-rich diets
column3: move towards more intensive agriculture/irrigation / unsustainable abstraction of water
column4: movement of water out of drainage basin eg aqueducts / upstream use by other countries
column5: growing of inappropriate/heavily water-dependent crops
column6: deforestation in drainage basin / pollution/contamination of water sources
column7: drought/reduction of rainfall (due to global warming/El NiÃ±o).

column1:
Award [1] for each correct factor identified, up to [3 max].
For last MP, do not accept simply a description of a regular climate eg low rainfall/dry season. The question asks for what will increase stress, so there needs to be a suggestion of change.
Marking points above may be stated as given or explicitly demonstrated through reference to examples/case studies (eg cotton around Aral Sea absorbs excessive water from an environment at risk).

The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although âESS terminologyâ has been conflated with âUnderstanding conceptsâ). This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band.

understanding concepts and terminology of equilibria, sustainability, natural capital/income, climatic factors (temp/precipitation/seasonality), greenhouse gases, climate change, biome shifts, water conservation, irrigation, desertification, vegetarian vs meat-rich diets, mitigation, adaptation, commercial vs artisanal, intensive vs extensive, food miles, selective breeding/genetic engineering, etc

breadth in addressing and linking influences of climate on food production eg water scarcity, shifting biomes, mean temperatures / precipitation, desertification, wind / rain / erosion, etc and influences of food production on climate eg methane production, deforestation, use of fossil fuels, global transport, etc and ways in which production strategies may adapt to, or mitigate climate change

examples of food production strategies that adapt to climate change eg water conservation, drip irrigation, terracing, drought/temperature resistant crops, aquaponics, greenhouses, etc and strategies that mitigate climate change eg switching from meat-rich diets, localising food production, employing artisanal/low-energy farming strategies, etc
balanced analysis of the extent to which production strategies from a range of contexts may contribute to, or mitigate against, an equilibrium between food production and the climate etc

a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given eg âalthough there are many production strategies that mitigate or adapt to climate change, the relationship has already shifted so far away from a sustainable equilibrium, and with growing populations, it seems unlikely that their contribution will be sufficient to avoid a tipping point in the futureâ

column1: With reference to Figure 9, explain why the highest levels of NOx are found in the centre of London.

<li>hence higher number of cars produces more NO<sub>x</sub>
<li>greater use of public transport/buses generates more NO<sub>x</sub> / use of diesel engines in buses produce more NO<sub>x</sub>
<li>traffic congestion means that cars generate more NO<sub>x</sub>

Examples of strategies include (N.B. no marks for stating strategy): use of congestion charges/higher tax for using cars, increase/shift to hybrid/electric cars, use of catalytic converters, increase public transportation / increase bicycles/free bicycle scheme, carpooling, shift to cleaner fuel sources for public transportation, stricter emissions controls on car exhaust, shift to solar powered cars/vehicles.

<li>reduction in number of deaths due to air pollution / increase in life expectancy

<li>reduced costs due to damage to monuments
<li>reduction in damage to plant tissue / increase in photosynthesis/plant growth/primary productivity
<li>increase in species diversity as high pollution levels have a detrimental effect on some species
<li>improves visibility in absence of smog/particulate pollution

Do not credit âless pollution would lead to greater tourism and increased revenuesâ.

column1: <li>pollution management may be achieved by reducing the manufacture and release of ozone-depleting substances

Outline the albedo effect and its role in regulating the Earthâs global temperature.
Compare and contrast the adaptation strategies to climate change for two societies.
Discuss whether biodiversity loss or climate change is a greater threat to human societies.
<li>albedo is a measure of the reflectiveness of a surface / is greater from lighter-coloured/smoother surfaces
<li>high albedo means that solar radiation is reflected away from a surface/reducing temperature / low albedo means that solar radiation is absorbed by a surface/increasing temperature
<li>the balance between the albedo of the Earthâs surfaces involves feedback loops
<li>negative feedback loops reduce change (retain the balance in ratio of albedo) and maintain the global temperature

<li>e.g. rise of global temperature <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased evaporation <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased cloud cover <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased albedo and reflection of solar radiation <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> decrease in global temperature
<li>a change in the balance can result in a positive feedback loop which amplifies changes and results in a rise in the Earthâs global temperature

<li>e.g. rise in global temperature <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increase melting in ice caps <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> decrease in albedo <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increase in solar radiation absorption <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> rise in global temperature
The following adaptation strategies can be credited provided it is clear whether they are common to both societies (compare) or are a point of difference (contrast).
<li>increase resilience of ecosystems, e.g. flood retention in mangroves/marshes
<li>planting of crops in previously unsuitable climates
<li>tree plantings, shady areas, reflective construction materials, green roofs, and the ecological management of rainwater
<li>capture indigenous knowledge for benefit of all
<li>invest/support scientific research into climate change monitoring and adaptation
<li>increase resilience of communities through economic development
<li>increased resilience of buildings and infrastructure (to heat waves, flooding)
<li>legislation and planning to consider climate impacts

Notes: Award [5 max] if only compare or only contrast used.
Award [3 max] if strategies are simply described but not clearly compared or contrasted between two named societies.

column2:
>Valid factors:
greater diversity of components/species increases resilience

column4: establishment of keystone species increases resilience
column5: larger storages/stores / more abundant/productive resources (nutrients, water, sunlight, reproductive rates, biomass etc) increase resilience (NB for credit, there must be indication of abundance in these resources, and if multiple examples are given like those in brackets, there is still only [1 max] allowed for this MP)
column6: larger size of the system increases resilience
column7: strong negative feedback systems increase resilience
column8: strong positive feedback mechanisms may decrease resilience

column10: a steady state equilibrium/balanced inputs and outputs (as in climax communities) increases resilience
column11: systems being close to a tipping point decrease resilience.

column2:
>Accept converse statements.
Award [1] for each correct factor identified, up to [4 max].
If valid factors are identified, but their effect on resilience is not, award [1] for each TWO factors up to [2 max] (ie FOUR factors). Eg identifying TWO factors and their effect on resilience, along with TWO factors but no specified effect would score [2+1=3] total OR eg TWO factors and their effect, along with ONE with no effect would score [2+0=2] total OR eg FOUR factors with no effect ([1+1=2] total).

column1:
A community of trees in a woodland has the following features of a system:
individuals/species of trees are the components of the system
column2: these components are interrelated/interdependent/form an integrated whole
column3: eg may regulate populations through competition / contribute to succession of community
column4: it has flows/transfers of matter/energy between components/storages
column5: eg leaf fall may provide nutrients through decomposition to other trees / pollination/genes/food storage in seeds / glucose is transported from leaves around tree
column6: components carry out processes/transformations
column7: eg photosynthesis/respiration/growth
column8: it is an open system exchanging matter and energy with surroundings
column9: eg absorption of solar energy / provision of nutrients for non-tree species (NB mark for either example of matter or energy, not both)
column10: it has feedback mechanisms to maintain equilibrium/balanced inputs and outputs
column11: eg more seed production â more competition between seedlings â fewer viable offspring / death of trees â more light entering canopy â more tree growth.

Please note: although ""quality of expression"" marking is no longer used in exams, this question from a past syllabus may still be useful for student practice.

Gases involved:
for GW are CO2/CH4/CFCs (whereas) for OD are halogen containing gases/CFCs/NOx

Human activities responsible:
for GW are human causes are very diverse (whereas) for OD more limited
column2: for GW include burning fossil fuels for transportation/agriculture/heating / rice culture / deforestation (whereas) for OD include refrigeration / spraying / cleaning electronics
for GW involves GHGs trapping more infra-red/heat (whereas) OD involves chemical breakdown of ozone molecules
column3: for GW involves increase in mean global temperature (whereas) OD involves more UV passing through atmosphere

column1:
Distribution:
GW occurs globally (whereas) OD is concentrated around the poles
column2: OD is caused largely by MEDC activities (whereas) LEDCs make significant contribution to GW through deforestation/rice culture.

column1: Please note: although ""quality of expression"" marking is no longer used in exams, this question from a past syllabus may still be useful for student practice.

column2: as coastal waters become warmer leads to an increase in productivity from phytoplankton
column3: but in some areas, waters may become too warm for locally adapted phytoplankton reducing productivity
column4: warmer waters hold less dissolved oxygen and may become less able to sustain larger organisms
column5: increase/decrease in productivity of phytoplankton will have the same impact on the entire ecosystem
column6: coastal ecosystems may contain coral reefs which are prone to bleaching in warmer temperature
column7: changing temperatures changes ecosystem characteristics and may allow invasive species to colonise an area
column8: sea level rise may cause coastal erosion
column9: change in ocean currents/El Nino patterns may bring in more nutrients increasing productivity / or the exact opposite: may deprive coastal areas from nutrient inflow thus decreasing productivity

column1:
Ozone depletion:
may be more important in southern oceans or northern oceans around coastlines as ozone hole is greater near the poles
column2: Increases mutation rates in phytoplankton changing ecosystem dynamics
column3: ozone depletion differential reduces productivity of phytoplankton (some more than others)
column4: reduced primary productivity has knock-on effects for entire ecosystems and may reduce population sizes of consumers and secondary productivity of ecosystem
column5: increased UV may cause health effects/reduce viability of marine animals e.g. young fish/shrimp larva/sea urchins living in coastal waters.

promote education about global warming as a way to change human behaviour causing the problem
column2: promote energy efficient strategies in order to reduce production of greenhouse gases (GHG)
column3: promote greater use of public transport / reduced flights / car sharing in order to reduce GHG production
column4: promote changes in diet to reduce meat consumption and thus reduce meat industryâs contribution to GHGs
column5: small communities, self-sufficiency / reduction of food miles, reduced consumerism - so less production GHG

column1:

financial incentives to change behaviour such as tax credits for using renewable energy/increasing household energy efficiency
column2: market based solutions such as carbon trading will incentivise companies to reduce carbon emissions
column3: legislation in the form of taxation on high carbon emissions
column4: legislation by government to reduce carbon emissions / e.g. international negotiated treaties/government targets/regional targets for carbon emissions
column5: community based initiatives such as meat free Mondays to reduce meat consumption and therefore community carbon footprint

column1:

promote adaptation to new conditions that result from global warming
column2: increase research and development for new fuels/renewable/nuclear energy / carbon capture technology

column4: promote development of new technologies to reduce carbon emissions such as more fuel efficient cars/electric cars/hybrid cars.

<li>albedo is a measure of the reflectiveness of a surface / is greater from lighter-coloured/smoother surfaces

<li>the balance between the albedo of the Earthâs surfaces involves feedback loops

<li>e.g. rise of global temperature <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased evaporation <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased cloud cover <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> increased albedo and reflection of solar radiation <math xmlns=""http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML""><mo>â</mo></math> decrease in global temperature

>Approximately 9 % of London’s land area is used for agriculture.
>Most commercial farms are on the edges of London, in the “green belt”.
>There is increasing interest in urban agriculture, such as vertical farming and rooftop gardening, including beekeeping.

column1: • Vertical farms exist underground and inside empty industrial buildings.
• They produce vegetables such as salad leaves, lettuce, spinach and herbs.
• Compared to traditional UK agriculture, vertical farms use up to 70 % less water and no pesticides.
• There is a higher density of bees in London than in rural areas of the UK.
• London’s bees have more diverse food sources and are healthier than rural bees.
• An 11 km “bee corridor” of wildflowers has been created in north London.

Outline the processes involved in the formation of fertile soils from bare rock.
Explain how negative and positive feedback mechanisms may influence the growth of decomposer populations in the soil.
To what extent are natural limiting factors more likely than population policies to limit global human population growth in the future?
(mechanical/chemical) weathering/breaking up of parent rock producing small particles
column2: deposition of sediment/eroded material (through wind/water) increasing soil depth
column3: dissolving of particle material to release soluble minerals
column4: colonization of parental rock/sediments by plants/animals/decomposers/pioneer species
column5: biological/atmospheric nitrogen fixation adds available nitrogen (nitrates, ammonium) to soil
column6: activity of mycorrhizal fungi/decomposition of dead organisms/leaf litter to release mineral/nutrients/organic material/humus
column7: growth of mosses form mats that stabilize soils in sterile/barren ecosystems
column8: earthworms/burrowing insects spread soil particles/open soil pores

column1: negative feedback occurs when the output of a process inhibits or reverses the same process / in such a way as to reduce change/counteract deviation/maintain equilibrium

positive feedback occurs when the output of a process accelerates that same process (will tend to amplify changes) / deviate away from equilibrium / or drive the system toward a tipping point (adopting a new equilibrium)

Negative feedback (occurring at carrying capacity):
growth of decomposer populations will reduce available organic material
column2: â¦thus leading to reduced growth in population
column3: growth of decomposer population may lead to increase in predators feeding on decomposers
column4: â¦thus leading to reduction in population growth

column1:
Positive feedback (occurring during phase of exponential growth):
increase in number of decomposers will increase potential for reproduction
column2: â¦thus leading to increased rate of population growth
column3: increase in decomposers may favourably modify environment (e.g. increase temperature/improve soil)
column4: â¦thus leading to increased rate of population growth

column1:

Award [2] max for each valid feedback loop correctly identified.
Only give credit if a complete loop is identified i.e. an increase of factor X leading eventually to a decrease or further increase of factor X (or the converse).
Do not credit simple changes in decomposer populations.

• Madagascar is the fourth largest island in the world. It is located in the Indian Ocean, off the eastern coast of Mozambique, Africa.
• In 2016, the crude birth rate was 32.1/1000 population and crude death rate was 6.7/1000 population.
• The main employment sectors are agriculture, fisheries and forestry.
• Exports include textiles, nickel and produce such as coffee, vanilla, sugar and shellfish.
<li>Areas of forest are being cleared for:
• Traditional farming â most of the population relies on traditional subsistence farming
• Timber â hardwoods such as ebony and rosewood are of high economic value
• Charcoal production â spiny bush wood is commonly used for charcoal production.
• Soil erosion is a serious problem in Madagascar. In some areas, up to 363 tonnes/ha/year of soil are being lost.

Outline how demographic tools can be used to study a human population.
Urban air pollution can become a problem as human populations develop. Evaluate urban air pollution management strategies at the three levels of intervention.
Examine the driving factors behind the changing energy choices of different countries using named examples.
demographic tools provide quantitative measures/indicators of changes occurring in the dynamics/growth of populations
column2: â¦and can be useful in making comparisons between populations / predictions of future changes
column3: Crude birth rate (CBR) is the number of live births per 1000 population per year / indicates rate at which births are occurring in a population
column4: Crude death rate (CDR) is number of deaths per 1000 population per year / indicates rate at which deaths are occurring in a population
column5: Total fertility rate (TFR) is the average number of children a woman would have in her lifetime in a given population / indicates the rate at which women are producing children
column6: Natural increase rate (NIR) is the crude birth rate minus the crude death rate / indicates the rate at which a population is growing (ignoring migrational changes)
column7: Doubling time (DT) is the number of years a population will take to double in size at its current rate of growth / indicates how quickly a population is growing compared to its current size

The resource booklet provides information on Algonquin Provincial Park in Canada. Use the resource booklet and your own studies to answer the following.
• By 1900 beavers had been hunted almost to extinction for their pelts (skin and fur).
• The IUCN classify beavers as âleast concernâ on the Red List.
• There are 19 registered trap lines in Algonquin Provincial Park where beavers may be trapped for their pelts by First Nation (indigenous) people.
• All trappers must have a licence, and may only trap during the hunting season using ethical traps.
• 135 000 beavers are still trapped and killed in Canada each year.
• The price for a beaver pelt was US$19 in 2015, compared to US$500 a pelt in 1940.
• Many landowners trap beavers on their property because of the damage they cause. (Beavers cut timbers and their dams can create floods causing millions of dollars of economic damage.)

The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band.

understanding concepts and terminology of sustainable harvesting/yield, natural capital/income, renewable vs non-renewable resources, reserves/protected areas, habitat vs species conservation, ecological pyramids, edge effects, design principles of conservation areas, ecotourism, local/national/international organisations, community support, etc

column1: • Scientists wanted to investigate how Mesquite might affect native wild mammals.

column1: • The three mines in the Swakop Valley require 10 million cubic metres of water a year. Groundwater aquifers now have very low volumes of water as demand from the mines and surrounding towns rises.
• The SEMP is based on studies that examine the effects of Uranium mining on the environment and communities of the Swakop Valley.
• The two tables below show the findings for two environmental indicators related to the effect of mining on water resources.

• The Erongo desalination plant was built to convert saltwater from the Atlantic Ocean into freshwater for a new mine called Trekkopje. The total production of freshwater from salt water is 20 million cubic metres a year.
• However after the Fukushima nuclear accident the demand for Uranium dropped and the Trekkopje mine did not open.

• The desalination plant now supplies water to the existing Uranium mines in the area, and has spare capacity that could be used to provide water to the communities in the Swakop Valley and to the proposed Husab mine.

To meet the minimum criteria for sustainability, a country needs to raise its human welfare above the threshold of high human development and have an ecological footprint below the Earthâs biocapacity.
Evaluate two strategies a country can implement to achieve the minimum criteria for sustainability.
ecological footprint (EF) is the reciprocal/inverse of carrying capacity (CC)
column2: populations with high per capita EF will have low CC / a population with a low EF would be less limited by CC in a given area (and vice versa) / EF determines whether a population is living within limits of CC
column3: EF identifies area needed (to satisfy the needs of a designated population) whereas CC identifies maximum population for sustainability (a designated area may sustain)
column4: EF is easier to calculate but has clear implications for CC
column5: both EF and CC depend upon rate of resource consumption/waste produced
column6: both EF and CC depend upon local environmental resources/waste processing ability
column7: both EF and CC facilitate a quantitative assessment of sustainability
column8: local EF may increase by import of goods produced elsewhere, whereas CC is dependent only to local productivity / CC may decrease through export of goods
column9: EF is not applied to non-human populations (whereas CC is difficult to apply to human populations).

column1: There is a huge range of potentially creditable responses/strategies for sustainability so do credit responses not listed below but of equivalent relevance and detail.
Strategy: reducing consumption of resources by change in lifestyle/reduced population growth/improved efficiency/imposing sustainable limits/ etc
column2: Advantage: addresses problem at the source / may simultaneously solve multiple problems / etc
column3: Disadvantage: may be unpopular / depends upon uncertain technological advance / may reduce economic development in LEDCs / etc

column1: Strategy: reducing pollution through reducing consumption / limiting/regulating emissions / cleaning-up/restoring polluted ecosystems / finding cleaner resources/alternatives / etc

column1: Using Figure 4, identify the country that is above the threshold for high human development and below the Earthâs biocapacity.

To meet the minimum criteria for sustainability, a country needs to raise its human welfare above the threshold of high human development and have an ecological footprint below the Earthâs biocapacity.
Evaluate two strategies a country can implement to achieve the minimum criteria for sustainability.
ecological footprint (EF) is the reciprocal/inverse of carrying capacity (CC)
column2: populations with high per capita EF will have low CC / a population with a low EF would be less limited by CC in a given area (and vice versa) / EF determines whether a population is living within limits of CC
column3: EF identifies area needed (to satisfy the needs of a designated population) whereas CC identifies maximum population for sustainability (a designated area may sustain)
column4: EF is easier to calculate but has clear implications for CC
column5: both EF and CC depend upon rate of resource consumption/waste produced
column6: both EF and CC depend upon local environmental resources/waste processing ability
column7: both EF and CC facilitate a quantitative assessment of sustainability
column8: local EF may increase by import of goods produced elsewhere, whereas CC is dependent only to local productivity / CC may decrease through export of goods
column9: EF is not applied to non-human populations (whereas CC is difficult to apply to human populations).
